entry.title: Re: Output Parameters for Handling Unit
entry.content:
Explore the below options

1) Custom Fields and Logic app
You can add a custom HU field (e.g., “Finalized Indicator”) using Custom Fields and Logic. This field can be exposed in Output Parameter Determination (OPD) as a condition parameter.

2) Released BAdIs (if available)
In Public Cloud, you can only implement SAP‑released BAdIs listed in the Extensibility Catalog. If there is a released BAdI for Handling Unit events (e.g., “HU placed in GI bin”), you can use it to set the custom field. If no such BAdI exists, you cannot inject custom ABAP logic — instead, you must rely on the standard OPD parameters (HU Step Completed, Storage Bin, Storage Process).

3) Fallback if no BAdI exists
Configure OPD rules directly and trigger HU_SHIPPING_LABEL only when HU Step Completed = true and Storage Bin = GI bin. This avoids multiple prints during intermediate HU changes, without needing a custom field.

entry.title: Re: unloading location timing is different
entry.content:
Why the mismatch happens is because of the following:

Delivery Note (DN)
Dates/times are stored in the ERP system (S/4HANA) in local time zone or user time zone.

Freight Order (FO)
When the DN is transferred to TM, the times are converted into the planning time zone defined in TM customizing.

If your ERP system and TM system (or the FO location master data) are set to different time zones, you will see exactly this kind of one‑hour offset.

So the recommended approach is

  • Verify the time zone maintained in the unloading location master data in TM. Align it with the DN’s plant/location time zone.
  • Check the planning time zone in TM customizing. If it differs from the business requirement, adjust it.
  • If you need FO times to always match DN times, ensure both systems (ERP and TM) use the same time zone for the relevant locations.

entry.title: Re: Clarifications on HANA DB access in SAP Private Cloud...
entry.content:
Hi Naveen,

Thank you very much for your detailed information.

It helps a lot. Further, i would like to know following points:

  1.  We completed the conversion of the Development system, and we now have access to the HANA DB (Private Cloud) through HANA Studio with our own USER ID. However, the DEV system has not yet been handed over to the ECS team for post-processing.
    • Currently, we have imported/exported HANA Calculation Views and created 10 HANA DB users along with their authorization roles/privileges in the Private Cloud DEV DB. Some issues are currently being fixed in the DEV DB.
    • Could you please clarify whether handing over the system for post-processing will remove the existing HANA DB users and authorization roles/privileges, or whether they will remain unchanged?
  2. We found an SAP note "2244289 - Information about HANA Repository Roles Creation in SAP Enterprise Cloud Services (ECS)" and it says follows;
    • "-Reason and Prerequisites

If an ECS customer wants to access the SAP HANA database, then they need to open a ticket in the SAP for Me to request access. After receiving the request, the SAP Enterprise Cloud Services Operations team (SAP ECS) will create an SAP HANA database user “CUST_USER_ROLE_ADMIN” on the customer’s database instance. CUST_USER_ROLE_ADMIN will allow the customer to create its own users and roles, and to assign the roles to its users. Hence in order to create the repository roles, ECS customers requires some guidelines or reference documents for handling such tasks.

My doubt is as per above sap note, will they remove all the HANA DB Users (In my case 10 HANA DB users) when they performs post-processing and give a User ID named "CUST_USER_ROLE_ADMIN" to re-create or configure Calculation views or MS Fabric integration from scratch ?

Thanks

Nishatharu

entry.title: Re: Two Way Match Supplier Criteria
entry.content:
Below are the recommended approaches for transition

Segment suppliers
Apply two‑way only to service vendors, recurring contracts, and low‑risk categories. Keep three‑way for high‑value, physical goods, or new suppliers.

Set tolerance thresholds
Define acceptable variances in PO vs invoice matching to reduce exceptions.

Automate controls
Use AP automation tools to enforce rules consistently and flag anomalies.

Monitor performance
Track disputes, overpayments, and fraud attempts after transition. Adjust supplier eligibility accordingly.

Communicate with suppliers
Ensure they understand the streamlined process and compliance expectations.

entry.title: Re: Data transfer between SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) and D...
entry.content:
Hi @aparna_surve1 ,

Although the blog from Ian which Nikhil is pointing towards provides functionality, the fact that consuming data via a Multi Action doesn't indicate that the use case you describe was meant to be served in this way natively.

If you want to build a sustainable solution I would recommend you to look at Business Data Cloud (BDC) and use Datasphere (DSP), (SAP) Databricks and SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) to manage your data in DSP, share data products with Databricks via Zero Copy Delta Sharing (and vice versa). You can then consume data via a live connection from SAC to DSP.

Kind regards,
Martijn van Foeken | Interdobs

entry.title: Re: System Requirements SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content: Hi Marco, I think, there is no definitive answer to this question. The requirements depend on the browser, network, operating system, other system loads, and the complexity of usage. As a rough guideline for basic access as a viewer, a modern computer with the latest version of Chrome or Edge, about 4 GB of RAM, and a standard dual-core processor should suffice. For a comfortable user experience, however, 8 GB of RAM or more, a current quad-core processor, and a stable, fast internet connection are recommended. Hard drive space is not an issue for the SAC. More important are a current operating system, sufficient browser cache, and a monitor with appropriate resolution, especially for complex stories.

entry.title: Re: Multiple Cost Objects in SES Despite No Cost Center i...
entry.content:
Why you are seeing multiple cost objects is because of the following:

Automatic account assignment derivation
Even if no cost center is in the upload file, the system can derive account assignments from:

  • The purchase order item (e.g., WBS element, internal order, network).
  • Default account assignment rules in configuration (e.g., valuation class, account determination).
  • Business rules in CPMM (Commercial Project Management) that link services to project objects.

Invoice integration
When the SES is transferred to invoice, the system re‑derives account assignments based on PO + SES + project settings. That is why you are seeing an additional cost object appear at invoice stage.

Check the following:

1) Purchase Order account assignment category

Verify whether the PO item is linked to a project (WBS, network) or another object. SES inherits this automatically.

2) Default account assignment settings
In Public Cloud, SSCUIs and configuration for account determination may insert defaults even if the upload file is blank.

3) Mass upload template mapping
Ensure the template fields are aligned with the intended account assignment category. Sometimes blank fields trigger fallback logic.

4) CPMM integration
If CPMM is active, SES items may be linked to project objects automatically, even without a cost center.

In short, the system is not “inventing” cost objects, it is deriving them from PO/CPMM rules when SES is created and invoiced. The fix usually involves tightening PO account assignment categories or adjusting CPMM integration so only the intended object is carried forward.

entry.title: Re: Impact Analysis (Change Record) – “Key is invalid” er...
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

I asked our internal chatbot for support and here's the response. Please check whether the information helps you solve your issue:

Technical BOM Identifier Requirement

  • Question: Does Impact Analysis with CDS A_BILLOFMATERIAL_1 require a technical BOM identifier (BillOfMaterial UUID) instead of the functional key (Material + Plant + Usage + Alternative)?
  • Answer: The CDS view A_BILLOFMATERIAL_1 might require a technical identifier like the BillOfMaterial UUID for precise mapping and execution. This is because the functional key might not uniquely identify the BOM in all scenarios, especially when dealing with complex BOM structures.

2. Limitations on BOM Usage

  • Question: Is there any standard limitation on BOM Usage (e.g., Engineering BOM not supported)?
  • Answer: There are no explicit limitations mentioned for BOM Usage in the context of Impact Analysis. However, ensure that the BOM Usage is correctly configured and supported within your system’s scope. For instance, some BOM usages might require specific configurations or activations, as seen with Engineering/Design BOMs in certain contexts.

3. Additional Configuration for BOM Key Mapping

  • Question: Is additional configuration required to map the BOM key correctly between Change Record UI and CDS view?
  • Answer: Yes, additional configuration might be necessary. Ensure that the UI Field Mapping Customization is correctly set up  to align with the CDS view’s requirements. This includes ensuring that all necessary fields are mapped and that the data types and formats are consistent. For more information, see Impact Analysis - Sample Scenario.

4. Known Issues or OSS Notes

  • Question: Are there known issues or OSS notes related to this error in version 2602?
  • Answer: While specific OSS notes for version 2602 are not mentioned, it is advisable to check the SAP Support Portal for any updates or notes related to Impact Analysis and BOM configurations. Additionally, ensure that all relevant business catalogs and authorizations are correctly assigned to avoid authorization errors.

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: Customer-specific Lot Sizing Procedure in ePP/DS
entry.content:
In standard SAP S/4HANA PP/DS heuristics (like SAP_PP_002), you cannot configure such a nuanced “fixed lot size + max lot size with merging logic” purely through Lot Sizing Procedures or Rounding Profiles. The standard lot sizing methods (fixed lot size, rounding value, minimum/maximum lot size, etc.) cover simpler rules, but they do not support the conditional merging you described (e.g., “if remainder < max – fixed, merge into last order, otherwise create a new one”).

So what you can do is check BAdI /SAPAPO/LOT_SIZE which is the standard extension point for custom lot sizing in PP/DS. Develop a custom lot sizing procedure and code the logic you described (fixed lot size, max lot size, conditional merging). Assign your custom procedure in the product master or heuristic settings, so it applies during planning runs.

entry.title: Re: How to add wait in SAP C4C Using ABSL Code
entry.content:
Hi Sri,

You can try the below approach for implementing a wait condition in ABSL. This should work:

import AP.PlatinumEngineering;

SessionContext.Wait(5);

The SessionContext.Wait() method pauses the execution for the specified duration before proceeding to the next statement. Please test it in your scenario and let us know if you encounter any issues.

Thanks.

entry.title: Building Custom Joule Capabilities: Message Types in Practice
entry.content:
• Introduction
  • Prerequisites
  • Message Types at a Glance
  • Plain Text Messages
  • Card Messages
  • List Messages
  • Carousel Messages
  • Media Messages
  • Buttons Messages
  • Likert Scale Messages
  • UI Integration Cards
  • Illustrated Messages
  • Common Pitfalls
  • UI Constraints and Limits
  • Try It Yourself
  • What is Next
  • Resources

Introduction

This post is part of the Joule Capability Development Blog Series. The first post covers setting up your development environment and building your first capability. This post builds on that foundation and dives into message types.

When building SAP Joule capabilities, the message: block in your function YAML controls how Joule renders the response. Choose the wrong type and a clean OData result becomes a wall of raw text. Choose the right one and the same data becomes a structured card, a list, or a rich UI5 Integration Card that feels like a native Fiori element.

Joule can also generate responses automatically using its LLM based on the data your function returns; see Gen AI Response Generation in the Joule Development Guide. The message: block gives you explicit control over the layout when the generated output is not structured enough for your use case.

The official Joule Development Guide documents the available message types, but it stops short of showing what each one looks like in practice or when to prefer one over another. This post fills that gap using the public Northwind OData service introduced in Building Custom Joule Capabilities: Getting Started. The examples here give a practical overview of each type and the decisions that go into choosing one over another.

Each message type has more fields and options than what fits in a single example, so treat this as a starting point rather than a complete reference. For the full SAP Help Documentation, see the Messages page of the Joule Development Guide. You can try the message types yourself using the digital assistants with their capabilities available in the respective folder of the GitHub Repo; see the Try It Yourself  section below.

One of the sample capabilities, Buttons, acts as a navigable launcher across all the others. Ask Joule to show different message types in action and you get one clickable postback button per message type. Clicking a button sends the right prompt back to Joule and triggers that capability without you having to type anything. You can read the sections in order or jump around in chat using the launcher.

 

Prerequisites

Complete Building Custom Joule Capabilities: Getting Started before starting here. You need the Joule CLI installed and a BTP destination named Northwind.

 

Message Types at a Glance

TypeWhat Joule rendersBest for
textPlain text, supports MarkdownFree-form answers, errors, simple facts
cardTitle, subtitle, image, status, descriptionSingle-record preview with optional drill-down
buttonsSummary text with clickable action buttonsGuided next-step selection
quickRepliesInline suggestion chips below a messageShort yes/no or option selection
carouselHorizontally scrollable set of cardsComparing a small set of records
listVertically scrollable list of itemsSearch results, multi-record display
mediaImage, video, or audio embedEmbedding a product demo, keynote, or how-to video
client_dataSilent payload for client-side logicTriggering navigation without visible output
ui5integrationCardFull UI5 Integration Card via manifest JSONRich Fiori-style grouped field display
illustrated_messageSAP illustration with title and descriptionSuccess/error/empty state feedback
likert_scaleClickable scale with labeled options (1–7)In-chat surveys and satisfaction ratings

This post covers text, card, buttons, list, carousel, media, ui5integrationCard, illustrated_message, and likert_scale. quickReplies is included in the table for completeness but not demonstrated separately, as it behaves like a lighter-weight buttons message.

 

Plain Text Messages

The text type is the simplest message type available. It renders the content string directly in the chat window and optionally interprets Markdown. Use it when you need a free-form answer, an error message, or a brief fact where visual structure would add no value.

# northwind_product_text/functions/get_product_text.yaml (relevant section)
- type: message
  message:
    type: text
    markdown: true
    content: |
      - **Product:** <? product.ProductName ?>
      - **Price:** $<? product.UnitPrice ?>
      - **In Stock:** <? product.UnitsInStock ?> units

Set markdown: true at the same level as content to render bold, italics, and lists. Add delay: <seconds> to introduce a pause before the message appears, useful when chaining multiple messages. For error states, a text message with a clear explanation is more useful to the user than an empty card.

Message Type: Text

Example use cases: single values, error messages, displaying markdown, or any response where structured layout would distract rather than help.

 

Card Messages

The card type renders a structured tile with a title, subtitle, icon or image, status badge, and a Markdown-enabled description block. It is the visual equivalent of a single-record detail page condensed into a chat message.

# northwind_product_card/functions/get_product_card.yaml (relevant section)
- type: message
  message:
    type: card
    content:
      title: <? product.ProductName ?>
      subtitle: "Unit Price: $<? product.UnitPrice ?>"
      imageUrl: "sap-icon://product"
      imageStyle: avatar
      description:
        value: "<? product.UnitsInStock ?> in stock · <? product.QuantityPerUnit ?>"
        markdown: false
      enableDetailView: false
      sections:
        - title: Stock Details
          attributes:
            - label: Units on Order
              value: <? product.UnitsOnOrder ?>
            - label: Reorder Level
              value: <? product.ReorderLevel ?>
            - label: Discontinued
              value: "<? product.Discontinued == true ? 'Yes' : 'No' ?>"

The sample above shows the core fields. Beyond those, the card type supports additional options: statusState accepts success, warning, error, and information, mapping to the standard SAP semantic colors (green, orange, red, blue). Set enableDetailView: true to make the card expandable. Add a buttons array under content to append action buttons directly below the card body, following the same structure described in the Buttons section.

Message Type: Card

When to use: any single-record result where the user benefits from seeing title, status, and a few key fields at a glance without leaving the chat.

 

List Messages

The list type renders a vertically scrollable container of items. Each item supports a title, subtitle, description, icon, and its own buttons. Use it for search results or any response that returns more than one record.

The list function accepts an optional category_id parameter. When provided it adds an OData $filter to the request, scoping results to a single category. When omitted it returns the first five products from the full catalog. This makes the same function reusable both as a standalone capability and as the target of a postback from the carousel described in the next section.

# northwind_product_list/functions/get_product_list.yaml
parameters:
  - name: category_id
    optional: true

action_groups:
  - condition: category_id != null
    actions:
      - type: api-request
        method: GET
        system_alias: NorthwindService
        path: "/Products?$filter=CategoryID%20eq%20<? category_id ?>&$select=ProductName,UnitPrice,UnitsInStock,QuantityPerUnit,UnitsOnOrder,ReorderLevel,Discontinued&$top=5"
        result_variable: api_result
  - condition: category_id == null
    actions:
      - type: api-request
        method: GET
        system_alias: NorthwindService
        path: "/Products?$select=ProductName,UnitPrice,UnitsInStock,QuantityPerUnit,UnitsOnOrder,ReorderLevel,Discontinued&$top=5"
        result_variable: api_result

The message block uses Handlebars to iterate the result array:

  - condition: api_result.status_code == 200 && api_result.body.value != null && api_result.body.value.size() > 0
    actions:
      - type: set-variables
        variables:
          - name: products
            value: <? api_result.body.value ?>
      - type: message
        scripting_type: handlebars
        message: >
          {
            "type": "list",
            "content": {
              "title": "Northwind Product Catalog",
              "subtitle": "Showing first 5 products",
              "enableDetailView": true,
              "elements": [
                {{#eachJoin products}}
                {
                  "title": "{{ProductName}}",
                  "subtitle": "${{UnitPrice}}",
                  "description": "{{UnitsInStock}} units in stock",
                  "imageUrl": "sap-icon://product",
                  "imageStyle": "avatar",
                  "sections": [
                    {
                      "title": "Stock Information",
                      "attributes": [
                        { "label": "Quantity per Unit", "value": "{{QuantityPerUnit}}" },
                        { "label": "Units on Order", "value": "{{UnitsOnOrder}}" },
                        { "label": "Reorder Level", "value": "{{ReorderLevel}}" }
                      ]
                    }
                  ]
                }
                {{/eachJoin}}
              ]
            }
          }

Handlebars is more advanced than SpEL, but it unlocks more powerful data manipulation. Most notably, it provides the ability to dynamically generate list elements from an array. The goal here is to take the products array returned by the OData API and turn each product into a list element, with its name as the title and its price as a detail attribute. Without Handlebars, you would need a fixed, hardcoded structure; with it, you can loop over however many products the API returns and build the elements array at runtime.

When scripting_type: handlebars is set, the entire message: block becomes a folded JSON string. The message type, content fields, and the elements array are all rendered by the Handlebars engine before Joule parses the result. {{#eachJoin products}} iterates the array and dynamically creates one list element per product. Use {{#eachJoin}} instead of the standard {{#each}} when building JSON arrays. It automatically handles comma separation between items, so you don't need the {{#unless @Last}},{{/unless}} workaround. Setting enableDetailView: true on the content makes each list item clickable, opening a detail panel on the right populated from the sections array on that element. Each section has a title and an attributes list of label/value pairs.

Message Type: List

When to use: search results, catalog browsing, or any multi-record response where the user needs to compare or scan across items.

 

Carousel Messages

The carousel type renders a horizontally scrollable row of cards. Unlike list, which stacks items vertically and supports expanded detail sections, carousel is optimized for quick visual comparison of a small set of entities. Each card has a title, subtitle, image, optional status, and buttons.

The sample capability fetches Northwind product categories and renders one card per category. Each card includes a postback button that, when clicked, sends a new utterance back to Joule to trigger the list capability filtered by that category's ID. This creates a two-step flow where you browse categories in the carousel, then drill into products for a selected category in a list.

# northwind_category_carousel/functions/get_categories_carousel.yaml
action_groups:
  - actions:
      - type: api-request
        method: GET
        system_alias: NorthwindService
        path: "/Categories?$select=CategoryID,CategoryName,Description"
        result_variable: api_result
  - condition: api_result.status_code == 200 && api_result.body.value != null && api_result.body.value.size() > 0
    actions:
      - type: set-variables
        variables:
          - name: categories
            value: <? api_result.body.value ?>
      - type: message
        scripting_type: handlebars
        message: >
          {
            "type": "carousel",
            "delay": 0,
            "content": {
              "enableDetailView": false,
              "cards": [
                {{#eachJoin categories}}
                  {
                    "title": "{{CategoryName}}",
                    "subtitle": "{{Description}}",
                    "imageUrl": "sap-icon://product",
                    "buttons": [
                      {
                        "type": "postback",
                        "title": "Explore {{CategoryName}}",
                        "text": "Explore {{CategoryName}}",
                        "value": "List products in category {{CategoryID}}"
                      }
                    ]
                  }
                {{/eachJoin}}
              ]
            }
          }

The postback button's value field, "List products in category {{CategoryID}}", is the utterance that Joule receives when the user clicks "Explore Beverages". The list capability's scenario description includes "list products in a specific category" and declares category_id as a slot, so Joule picks it up and extracts the numeric ID from that utterance. The list function then applies $filter=CategoryID%20eq%20<? category_id ?> to the OData request.

Message Type: Carousel

When to use: a small, fixed set of entities where side-by-side comparison is useful and each item has a natural follow-on action, such as drilling into a filtered view.

 

Media Messages

The media type embeds an image or video directly in the chat window. For video, Joule supports YouTube, Vimeo, Daily Motion, SAP Video, and SAP TV Video URLs. For direct file links, supported formats are MP4, WEBM, OGV, and 3GP. All URLs must be publicly accessible.

The sample capability for this section is intentionally simple, with no API call, no parameters, and no slots. It demonstrates that a capability does not need to reach a backend service to be useful. The function sends a short text message followed by the video embed in a single unconditional action group.

# sapphire_2026_media/functions/get_video.yaml
action_groups:
  - actions:
      - type: message
        message:
          type: text
          content: "Here's the SAP SAPPHIRE 2026 keynote."
      - type: message
        message:
          type: media
          content: "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CocpyxAizwE"

The two message actions in the same action group send back-to-back. The text message appears first, giving the embed context before the video player renders below it. This chaining pattern applies to any message type combination: you can follow a card with a buttons message, or a text summary with a list of results, by placing multiple message actions in the same action group.

The content field takes the URL directly as a string without additional nesting required. For a YouTube URL, Joule renders an embedded player. The user can play the video without leaving the chat.

Message Type: Media

When to use: product demo videos, how-to recordings, keynote highlights, or any response where a visual or audio asset communicates more than text alone.

 

Buttons Messages

The buttons type renders one or more clickable action buttons, optionally preceded by a summary text. It is the right choice when you want to give the user a clear set of next steps directly in the response, or when a single result can be explored in multiple ways.

This capability is the launcher for the rest of the post. Ask Joule to "show me different Joule message types in action" and it renders one postback button per message type. Clicking a button sends the right prompt back to Joule and triggers that capability without the user having to type anything.

# northwind_product_buttons/functions/show_message_type_launcher.yaml
action_groups:
  - actions:
      - type: message
        message:
          type: buttons
          content:
            title: "Pick a message type to see it in action:"
            buttons:
              - type: postback
                title: See as text
                value: "Show me product Chai as text"
              - type: postback
                title: See as card
                value: "Show me product Chai as a card"
              - type: postback
                title: Browse all products via list
                value: "Provide me a list of products"
              - type: postback
                title: See categories as carousel
                value: "Show me Northwind categories"
              - type: postback
                title: See products as integration card
                value: "Show me products as an integration card"
              - type: postback
                title: See as illustrated message
                value: "Show me product Chai as an illustrated message"
              - type: postback
                title: See as media (SAPPHIRE video)
                value: "What happened at SAPPHIRE 2026"
              - type: postback
                title: Rate Joule (Likert)
                value: "Rate my Joule experience"

The function has no parameters, no slots, and no API call. Chai is hardcoded in each postback value that requires a product name, so every downstream capability receives a valid input when the button is clicked.

Some button types available in Joule:

  • postback: sends the value string back to Joule as a new user message, continuing the conversation
  • navigation: opens a URL in the browser or navigates within the host application
  • clientdata: passes a silent payload to the host application without sending a visible message

Message Type: Buttons

When to use: after delivering a result when the logical next action is predictable, or when a single entity can be viewed in multiple ways and you want to let the user choose without typing follow-up prompts.

 

Likert Scale Messages

The likert_scale type renders a row of labeled, clickable scale options directly in the chat. It is designed for in-chat surveys and feedback collection where you need a numeric rating on a defined scale. When the user clicks an option, the selected value is stored in a slot and becomes available to downstream steps in the same dialog function.

The supported range is 1 to 7 options. The compiler does not enforce contiguous values or prevent duplicates, which gives you flexibility to configure non-standard scales. In practice a simple 1-to-5 or 1-to-7 Likert scale is the most common use case.

The sample capability asks the user to rate how well Joule fits their needs, stores the response in a slot, and sends a personalised thank-you text message.

The scenario declares the slot so Joule knows to wait for the Likert response before advancing:

# northwind_product_likert/scenarios/joule_satisfaction.yaml
description: >-
  Capture the user's satisfaction with Joule. Use this when the user
  wants to rate their experience or answer a survey question about Joule.

slots:
  - name: joule_fit_for_purpose
    description: >-
      The user's rating of whether Joule does what they need, on a scale
      from 1 (Strongly disagree) to 5 (Strongly agree).

target:
  type: function
  name: scenario_joule_survey

The function sends the scale first, then branches on the captured slot value:

# northwind_product_likert/functions/scenario_joule_survey.yaml (relevant section)
action_groups:
  - condition: joule_fit_for_purpose == null
    actions:
      - type: message
        message:
          type: likert_scale
          content:
            prompt: "Joule does what I need it to do"
            options:
              - label: "Strongly disagree"
                value: 1
              - label: "Disagree"
                value: 2
              - label: "Neutral"
                value: 3
              - label: "Agree"
                value: 4
              - label: "Strongly agree"
                value: 5
  - condition: joule_fit_for_purpose != null
    actions:
      - type: message
        message:
          type: text
          content: "Thank you! You rated Joule's fit for purpose as <? joule_fit_for_purpose ?> out of 5."

The first action group fires when the slot is empty, displaying the scale. Once the user clicks an option, the slot is populated and Joule re-enters the function, this time matching the second action group and sending the confirmation text.

A few practical notes:

  • The likert_scale message type is designed to work with slot filling. Declare the slot in the scenario so Joule waits for the user's selection before proceeding.
  • The prompt field is the question text displayed above the scale. Keep it short, one sentence.
  • options requires at least one entry and at most seven. Values do not need to be contiguous.
  • The selected value is stored as a number in the slot, so downstream SpEL comparisons use numeric operators, such as joule_fit_for_purpose >= 4.

Message Type: Likert Scale

When to use: in-chat feedback forms, satisfaction surveys, or any scenario where you need a structured numeric rating from the user without leaving the chat.

 

UI Integration Cards

How it differs from a plain card message

The card message type is a fixed Joule UI component with a predefined layout. It has a title, subtitle, image, status, and description. You populate fields but you cannot change the layout itself.

The ui5integrationCard type embeds a full UI5 Integration Card driven by a JSON manifest. You control the card type, the column layout, field grouping, labels, and semantic coloring. The trade-off is verbosity: the manifest is considerably longer than a card message, and all data must come from the dialog function. The card manifest cannot reach out to a BTP destination directly.

Card types available

The UI5 Integration Card framework supports a range of card types, each suited to different data shapes and presentation needs:

Card typeBest for
ObjectSingle-record detail view with grouped label/value fields
TableMulti-record tabular display with columns, alignment, and state badges
ListCompact vertically stacked items with icon, title, description
AnalyticalCharts: bar, line, donut, bullet, stacked bar
TimelineChronological event sequences
CalendarDate-based scheduling views
ComponentCustom UI5 component embed (not supported in Joule)

The full catalog with live examples is in the UI5 Integration Card Explorer.

The Table card

The Table card is well suited for multi-record results. It renders columns with headers, supports right-alignment for numeric values, and displays semantic state badges with icons on any column using state, showStateIcon, and inverted.

The sample capability fetches up to 10 products with their category name by extending the Category entity, as well as the stock status, and renders them as a table with four columns: Product, Category, In Stock, and Status.

The key pattern is a two-step construction:

  1. Build the rows array with Handlebars, computing status and statusState per row inline using {{#if}} comparisons against UnitsInStock and Discontinued
  2. Inject the rows into the manifest via SpEL and send it as a message action
# northwind_product_integration_card/functions/get_product_integration_card.yaml
# ... (parameters and api-request action groups follow the same pattern as earlier examples)
  - condition: api_result.status_code == 200 && api_result.body.value != null && api_result.body.value.size() > 0
    actions:
      - type: set-variables
        variables:
          - name: products
            value: <? api_result.body.value ?>
      - type: set-variables
        scripting_type: handlebars
        variables:
          - name: tableRows
            value: >
              [
                {{#eachJoin products}}
                {
                  "product": "{{ProductName}}",
                  "category": "{{Category.CategoryName}}",
                  "unitsInStock": "{{UnitsInStock}}",
                  "status": "{{#if Discontinued}}Discontinued{{else if (eq UnitsInStock 0)}}Out of Stock{{else if (lt UnitsInStock 10)}}Low Stock{{else}}In Stock{{/if}}",
                  "statusState": "{{#if Discontinued}}Error{{else if (eq UnitsInStock 0)}}Error{{else if (lt UnitsInStock 10)}}Warning{{else}}Success{{/if}}"
                }
                {{/eachJoin}}
              ]
      - type: message
        scripting_type: spel
        message: >
          {
            "type": "ui5integrationCard",
            "content": {
              "_version": "1.17.0",
              "sap.app": { "type": "card", "id": "northwind.product.tablecard" },
              "sap.card": {
                "type": "Table",
                "header": {
                  "title": "Northwind Products",
                  "subtitle": "Showing up to 10 products"
                },
                "content": {
                  "data": { "json": <? tableRows ?> },
                  "maxItems": 10,
                  "row": {
                    "columns": [
                      { "title": "Product",   "value": "{product}",      "identifier": true },
                      { "title": "Category",  "value": "{category}" },
                      { "title": "In Stock",  "value": "{unitsInStock}", "hAlign": "End" },
                      {
                        "title": "Status",
                        "value": "{status}",
                        "state": "{statusState}",
                        "showStateIcon": true,
                        "inverted": true
                      }
                    ]
                  }
                }
              }
            }
          }

A few points worth noting:

  • $expand=Category fetches the related Category entity in a single OData call.
  • data.json placement: in Table cards, data sits inside content, not at the sap.card level. The row.columns then use {product}, {category} etc. as binding paths into the JSON array.
  • State badges: "state": "{statusState}" with "showStateIcon": true and "inverted": true produces the filled colored badge with icon seen in the UI5 Card Explorer examples. Valid state values are Success, Warning, Error, Information, and None.
  • Two-step construction: Handlebars builds tableRows as a JSON array string, then SpEL injects it into the manifest via <? tableRows ?>. This is necessary because the YAML schema validator rejects SpEL expressions in positions that expect a literal array.

Message Type: UI Integration Card

When to use: multi-record results where column layout, numeric alignment, or semantic state coloring adds clarity that a plain list message cannot provide. For a single-record detail view, the Object card type is a better fit. The full range of UI5 card types, Analytical charts, Timeline, Calendar, opens up significant display possibilities beyond what Joule's built-in message types support.

Key restrictions for ui5integrationCard in Joule:

  • Only static data bindings are supported. You cannot declare a BTP destination inside the manifest and have Joule fetch data from it at render time.
  • Action handlers defined in the manifest are not executed by Joule.
  • Component card types are not supported.
  • Filters and user-defined manifest parameters are not supported.

 

Illustrated Messages

The illustrated_message type renders an SAP illustration alongside a title, subtitle, and up to two call-to-action buttons. It is designed for the moments that bookend a transaction or search, such as a success confirmation, a not-found state, or a system error. Rather than showing a blank text response after an action, it gives the user a visual signal that something meaningful happened.

The sample capability looks up a Northwind product and branches into two states based on what the API returns:

  • Product found, in stock, and not discontinued: Success illustration
  • Product found but discontinued or out of stock: UnableToLoad illustration
  • Product not found at all: tntEmpty illustration
# northwind_product_illustrated/functions/get_product_illustrated.yaml (relevant section)
  - condition: api_result.status_code == 200 && api_result.body.value != null && api_result.body.value.size() > 0
    actions:
      - type: set-variables
        variables:
          - name: product
            value: <? api_result.body.value[0] ?>
      - type: message
        message:
          type: illustrated_message
          content:
            illustration_name: "<? (product.Discontinued == true || product.UnitsInStock == 0) ? 'UnableToLoad' : 'TntSuccess' ?>"
            title: "<? product.ProductName ?>"
            subtitle: "$<? product.UnitPrice ?> · <? product.UnitsInStock ?> units in stock"
            buttons:
              - title: Look up another product
                type: postback
                text: Look up another product
                value: "Show me another product as an illustrated message"
              - title: Browse catalog
                type: navigation
                navigation_target:
                  url: "https://services.odata.org/V3/Northwind/Northwind.svc/Products"
  - condition: api_result.status_code != 200 || api_result.body.value == null || api_result.body.value.size() == 0
    actions:
      - type: message
        message:
          type: illustrated_message
          content:
            illustration_name: NoEntries
            title: "Product not found"
            subtitle: "No product matching '<? product_name ?>' was found in the Northwind catalog."
            buttons:
              - title: Try again
                type: postback
                text: Try again
                value: "Show me another product as an illustrated message"

The illustration_name is a SpEL expression that evaluates to UnableToLoad when the product is discontinued or out of stock, and TntSuccess otherwise. The not-found branch uses the separate NoEntries illustrated message.

The buttons array supports the same button types as the card and buttons message types: postback, navigation, phonenumber, and clientdata. The postback button's value field is the utterance Joule receives when the user clicks. Note it includes "as an illustrated message" to keep Joule routing to this capability rather than a different one for the next lookup.

A few practical notes:

  • For inspiration on illustration names, browse the UI5 Web Components IllustratedMessageType. To see how they look, check the UI5 IllustratedMessage samples.
  • The maximum number of buttons is two.

Message Type: Illustrated Message

When to use: after a mutation (create, update, delete) to confirm the outcome, or when a search returns no results and you want to guide the user toward a recovery action rather than leaving them with a silent empty state.

 

Common Pitfalls

A few mistakes that are easy to make when building capabilities for the first time:

  • URL-encode dynamic values in OData paths. Spaces in a user-supplied slot value (e.g. "Chai Tea") will break an OData filter if embedded directly. Add a set-variables step first with <? product_name.urlEncode() ?>, then reference the encoded variable in the path.
  • Use {{#eachJoin}} instead of {{#each}} when building JSON arrays in Handlebars. {{#eachJoin}} inserts commas between items automatically. {{#each}} does not, which produces invalid JSON.
  • In Table integration cards, data belongs inside content, not at the sap.card level. Placing it at the wrong level causes the card to render empty.
  • likert_scale stores the selected value as a number. Downstream SpEL comparisons must use numeric operators (>=, ==), not string comparisons.
  • Postback button value strings must match the target capability's scenario description. Joule uses the value as an utterance for intent matching. Include the keywords that route to the right capability.

 

UI Constraints and Limits

Each message type enforces hard limits on element counts and text lengths. Exceeding these silently truncates or hides content. The full tables covering element counts, character limits, full-screen widths, and markdown variants are in the UI Constraints and Limits page of the Joule Development Guide.

 

Try It Yourself

The complete sample files are in this GitHub folder. The assistant is structured as one Digital Assistant file referencing nine separate capability folders, one per message type. Each capability's scenario description includes a phrase like "as text", "as a card", or "as a list". Joule then uses these during intent matching to route the incoming prompt to the correct capability. Once deployed, ask for any Northwind product with buttons to get a clickable launcher that triggers each message type without typing the prompts manually.

Deploy everything in one step:

cd Blog_01_joule_message_types
joule deploy -c -n "northwind_message_types"
joule launch "northwind_message_types"

Try each of these prompts to exercise all nine message types:

Message typeSample prompt
Text"Show me product Chai as text"
Card"Show me product Chai as a card"
Buttons"Show me different Joule message types in action"
List"Provide me a list of products"
Carousel"Show me Northwind categories"
UI Integration Card"Show me products as an integration card"
Illustrated Message"Show me product Chai as an illustrated message"
Likert Scale"Rate your Joule experience"
Media"Show me the SAPPHIRE 2026 keynote"

For the carousel, click the "Explore" button on any category card to trigger the list capability filtered by that category. The list capability also works standalone. "List products in category 1" will return only Beverages products directly.

For the illustrated message, try a product that exists ("Show me product Chai as an illustrated message") to see the success state, and a name that matches nothing ("Show me product XYZ as an illustrated message") to see the not-found empty state.

What is Next

In the next post we look at restricting which scenarios are visible to which users using IAS group membership and BTP role assignments. See Building Custom Joule Capabilities: Role-Based Scenario Access in Joule. More posts in this series are on the way.

Resources

  • Messages — Joule Development Guide
  • UI Integration Cards — Joule Development Guide
  • UI5 Integration Card Explorer

entry.title: Re: From Blueprint to Reality: SAP BDC Data Architecture Deep Dive Miniseries- June 2026
entry.content: Getting error message like "It looks like you don't have necessary permission". please help me.

entry.title: Re: Issue in Milestone Activity Confirmation at Order Level
entry.content:
##GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

I asked our internal chatbot for support and here's the response. Please check whether it helps you solve the issue:

Milestone Confirmation Issue in Production Orders

When using milestone confirmation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, the expected behavior is that confirming a milestone operation should automatically confirm all preceding operations along with their respective activity parameters. However, you are experiencing an issue where only the last operation’s activity parameters are confirmed, while earlier operations remain unconfirmed.

Expected Behavior

  • Milestone Confirmation Logic: Once a milestone operation is confirmed, all preceding operations should be automatically confirmed, including their activity parameters. For more information, see Production Planning and Control - Milestone Confirmation.

Actual Behavior

  • Issue: Only the last operation’s activity parameters are confirmed, while earlier operations remain unconfirmed.

Possible Causes and Resolutions

  • Configuration Settings: Ensure that the configuration settings for milestone confirmations are correctly set. The system should be configured to automatically confirm preceding operations when a milestone operation is confirmed.
  • Control Key Settings: Verify that the control keys for the operations are correctly marked for milestone confirmation. Operations need to be defined as milestone operations in their control keys.

Example

  • Scenario: You have a production order with multiple operations, and the last operation is marked for milestone confirmation. Upon confirming this operation, all preceding operations should be confirmed automatically.

KInd regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: Material Documents Overview (F1077): Enhancing Features and Usability

Hi Darren,

thanks so much for your feedback!

To your questions:

1.) Copying information, in general it is possible to also copy information e. g. the material code and to paste it in. Your use case is not quite clear to me, do you mean mass copying?

2.) Super question - we have now updated the app with further information - please check out the new variants under "My views": 

The list with values will show the stock value change but also signed quantity and quantity change in color code. The variants flat list and hierarchy list are taken from MB51 and show the report in the exact same layout. If you want to always see this view, when the app starts, save this version as default.

entry.title: Prasad Khandavalli
entry.content:
More about Prasad Khandavalli in SAP Community

 

 


Name: Prasad Khandavalli
Location: Bangalore, India
Occupation: BTP Solution Adoption Advisor at SAP


Prasad, tell us about yourself.
Who are you, what do you like to do, what is important to you?

I’m PVSB Prasad, part of the SAP BTP Adoption and Consumption team, where I engage with customers globally to drive awareness and accelerate the adoption of SAP BTP technologies. With a background in SAP CX, I focus on extending capabilities across CX and BTP to enable end-to-end, integrated implementations that deliver real business value. I enjoy exploring new technologies, sharing knowledge, and helping teams and customers adopt innovative solutions. Continuous learning, collaboration, and creating meaningful impact through technology are what matter most to me. 

Blog – Read my posts: I actively share insights, learnings, and real-world experiences through my blog contributions in the SAP Community. Outside of work, I enjoy swimming and playing badminton, which help me stay active and maintain a balanced lifestyle. 


What is your area of expertise?
What does a usual day at work look like for you?

My area of expertise lies in SAP BTP and SAP CX, with hands-on experience across solutions such as Service Cloud and Sales Cloud V2, SAP C4C, Commerce, SAP S/4HANA Cloud, and Qualtrics, along with BTP services like SAP Build Apps, BTP Document Extraction, SAP AI Launchpad, SAP Event Mesh, SAP Translation Hub, and DQM Services. 

I focus on enabling end-to-end, scalable, and intelligent enterprise solutions while driving the adoption of SAP BTP capabilities. This experience allows me to support customers in building integrated and future-ready solutions. A typical day involves architecture discussions, providing technical and strategic advisory, and engaging with global stakeholders to drive successful SAP BTP adoption and consumption. I also dedicate time to mentoring and coaching team members, while actively contributing to the SAP Community through knowledge sharing and collaboration. 


What inspired you to become an SAP Champion in SAP Community?

The SAP Community played a key role in my early learning journey — through blog posts, answered questions, and real-world insights from practitioners. Contributing back felt like a natural step. Becoming an SAP Champion reflects that ongoing commitment to share knowledge, stay engaged, and support others. To me, it’s not just a recognition — it’s a responsibility to remain active, helpful, and contribute to the growth of the community. 


How do you learn in SAP Community?
What is your daily/weekly routine in SAP Community?

SAP Community is my go-to platform for staying current. I regularly follow blog posts from experts and peers, participate in Q&A discussions, and explore SAP Discovery Center for hands-on learning. One of the most effective ways I learn is by reading community questions—they often highlight real-world challenges that go beyond standard documentation. 

On a regular basis, I answer questions, share insights from my experience, and engage in discussions. I also contribute through webinars, sessions, and content that help others adopt new SAP technologies. 


What is your suggestion to get the most out of SAP Community?

Don’t just consume, contribute. Ask questions, share your learnings, and engage actively. Even simple contributions can create value for others. Follow relevant topics and manage tags to personalize your feed and stay updated. Most importantly, build connections—SAP Community is not just a knowledge platform, but a network of supportive and knowledgeable professionals. 

Prasad’s question to you: What is one practical learning or experience from SAP Community that directly helped you solve a real project challenge?

entry.title: Masashi Otsuka
entry.content:
More about Masashi Otsuka in SAP Community

 

 


Name: Masashi Otsuka
Location: Tokyo, Japan
Occupation: Enterprise Transformation at PWC


Masashi, tell us about yourself.
Who are you, what do you like to do, what is important to you?

I am an SAP consultant based in Japan, with around 20 years of experience working on SAP projects. Throughout my career, I have been involved in the design, development, and implementation of SAP-based business systems. Currently, I work across multiple SAP projects, supporting technical decision-making and delivery quality, mainly around SAP S/4HANA and SAP BTP. What is important to me is not the technology itself, but whether a solution can be used sustainably in real business operations and can be continuously improved over time. I enjoy organizing lessons learned from real projects and sharing them with others, helping teams and projects improve overall quality. 


What is your area of expertise?
What does a usual day at work look like for you?

My main areas of expertise are SAP S/4HANA, SAP BTP, ABAP and ABAP Cloud, and Clean Core extension strategies. I am especially involved in side-by-side development, interface design, and technical quality reviews and standardization. Rather than optimizing a single project, I focus on creating reusable approaches and best practices that can be applied across multiple projects. 

In my daily work, I support multiple SAP projects in parallel, reviewing designs, and discussing technical decisions with project teams. I often work closely with teams to identify quality risks early and to find better design and development approaches together. I am also involved in internal Center of Excellence (CoE) activities, where we identify common challenges across projects and turn project lessons into standards and guidelines. A large part of my work is bringing real project experience back to the CoE and sharing it with other teams. I try to balance supporting individual projects and improving technical quality at an organizational level. 


What inspired you to become an SAP Champion in SAP Community?

I have been actively involved in SAP community activities in Japan for many years, sharing knowledge from real projects through events, sessions, and information sharing. I wanted to use SAP Community as a way to share those experiences more broadly and to connect with the global community. At the same time, I am interested in bringing global discussions, ideas, and trends back into projects and community activities in Japan. 

During this journey, seeing SAP Champions like Mio Yasutake also became one of the motivations that encouraged me to take this step. As an SAP Champion, I would like to continue contributing as a bridge between local and global communities. 


How do you learn in SAP Community?
What is your daily/weekly routine in SAP Community?

I mainly learn through blog posts, Q&A, and event sessions in SAP Community. I especially focus on practical topics such as Clean Core, SAP BTP, and new SAP policies and directions. In Japan, I am also involved in organizing SAP Inside Track Tokyo, and discussions and insights from these events are an important part of my learning as well. Learning how other practitioners think and make decisions is something I truly value in the community. 

On a weekly basis, I check blog posts and discussions related to topics I am interested in. 
When I find useful insights, I share them internally, reflect them in presentations, or use them in project and community activities. I try to make sure that what I learn does not stay personal, but is shared with teams and the wider community. 


What is your suggestion to get the most out of SAP Community?

My suggestion is to start with real challenges from your own project. Search, read, ask questions, and share what you learn—even small insights can be helpful for others. SAP Community becomes much more valuable when you participate, not just read. As a first step, why not start by simply clicking “Like” on an article you find useful? 
So yes—please give this post a Like, too 😊

entry.title: Simone Azevedo
entry.content:
More about Simone Azevedo in SAP Community

 


Name: Simone Azevedo
Location: Curitiba, Brazil
Occupation:Technical SAP Business Process Analyst at ExxonMobil (via Pantheon Inc) | SAP MM Consultant


Simone, tell us about yourself.
Who are you, what do you like to do, what is important to you?

I consider myself a highly dedicated person. Whenever I commit to something, I give my very best. Today, I am a mother, a wife, and a professional who truly enjoys working with SAP. Over the past 10 years, my journey with SAP has been both challenging and deeply fulfilling. Along this path, especially after becoming a mother, I learned how important it is to take care of ourselves and seek balance in life. That’swhy I value opportunities that allow me to maintain a good quality of life while continuing to grow professionally. 

I strongly believe that nothing happens by chance. My faith has always guided me through important decisions and challenges, giving me strength and clarity throughout my journey. 


What is your area of expertise?
What does a usual day at work look like for you?

My area of expertise is SAP Material Management (MM), supported by a strong background in Supply Chain. This foundation has given me a solid understanding of end-to-end supply chain processes, which hasbeen essential in my transition and growth within SAP MM. 

On a typical day, I support the business by gathering requirements from stakeholders, analyzing their needs, and translating them into effective SAP solutions. I focus on identifying the best approach for each scenario, ensuring process efficiency, system reliability, and alignment with business objectives. 


What inspired you to become an SAP Champion in SAP Community?

When I first started working with SAP, I deeply admired the SAP Mentors who were always willing to share their knowledge and support others. Their openness and generosity truly inspired me. That inspiration became real when I met @Marssel700 and @FMotter, SAP Mentors and organizers of SAP Inside Track São Paulo. Through their encouragement, I had the idea of bringing the event to Curitiba. They supported me throughout the entire journey, and I am truly grateful for their trust and partnershi 

Today, I am part of the team that organizes several community events, with a special focus on initiatives for women in SAP. This journey has shown me how one person in the SAP Community can inspire othersand create meaningful impact. I want to continue inspiring, engaging, and empowering people — and becoming an SAP Champion would help amplify my voice and give greater visibility to these community efforts. 


How do you learn in SAP Community?
What is your daily/weekly routine in SAP Community?

I learn from the SAP Community almost every day. Community members consistently share valuable experiences and insights that help me stay updated and continuously improve. As an event organizer, I pay close attention to posts that present new ideas, trends, and best practices that we can apply to our local initiatives. I strongly believe that a successful event must provide a great experience for everyone involved — sponsors, attendees, and speakers alike. When the experience is positive for all, people naturally want to participate again. 

Together with the other curators in Curitiba, we work to deliver the best possible experience using the resources available to us. 


What is your suggestion to get the most out of SAP Community?

The key is active participation. Don’t be afraid to ask questions, introduce yourself, or share your thoughts and experiences. Engage with the content, contribute when you can, and attend SAP Community events whenever possible. The more you engage, the more you learn — and the more meaningful connections you build. 

Simone's question to you: When did the SAP Community truly make a difference in your journey?

entry.title: Xavier Le Garrec
entry.content:
More about Xavier Le Garrec in SAP Community

 

Name: Xavier Le Garrec
Location: Lives in San Francisco, USA but originally from Lorient, France 
Occupation: HCM Services North America (Reward to Retain & AI) and Customer Engagement Principal at SAP

 


Xavier, tell us about yourself.
Who are you, what do you like to do, what is important to you?

My name is Xavier and I have been working as an implementation partner on SAP SuccessFactors modules for about 15 years. What is important to me is empowering customers and other experts to deliver great results in actual projects or in terms of knowledge sharing. At SAP I enjoy networking across business lines to find out and document things that can be useful for all our customers. 


What is your area of expertise?
What does a usual day at work look like for you?

I am a subject matter expert for the Compensation Module of SuccessFactors but I am also certified on Performance Management and Employee Central modules. Last year I became a member of the AI Adoption team at SAP which has helped me become knowledgeable on SAP Business AI as well. 

Every morning I open my email inbox and look at my Daily Digest from the community to know how much has been published overnight on topics I follow. If there is content, then I start my day by answering there then move on to customer meetings. When I am not sure about the answer to a question on the community, I log into my demo environment to try to replicate it and provide the best answer possible based on hands-on experimentation. 

I mostly work from home but enjoy riding my bike for 20 minutes to the SAP office once a week. 


What inspired you to become an SAP Champion in SAP Community?

In 2020 I started writing blog posts because I realized the knowledge gained from specific pain points encountered in implementations was often lost for others to use unless we documented them. Knowledge Base Articles only supplemented implementation guides but didn't help answer critical questions such as leading practices on specific topics or possible workarounds to known limitations. 

In 2025 after writing over 60 blogs and answering thousands of questions on the community I realized I had become the top kudoed author in the Human Capital Management category on the SAP Community. That same year I started following @Martin-Pankraz onLinkedin and saw it said "SAP Champion" on his profile header and looked up the program and thought to myself it would be great to be able to be an active participant in this great team. 


How do you learn in SAP Community?
What is your daily/weekly routine in SAP Community?

I start every day by answering questions raised on the "SAP SuccessFactors Compensation" tag then go on to explore additional tags or blogs.  


What is your suggestion to get the most out of SAP Community?

Save the pages/tags you are interested in in your browser's Favorites so you can look at new content created overnight (blog or help requests) on a daily basis. Tips and Tricks to start writing on the SAP Blog (name of the blogging platform before it was merged with the community) was written by me and I encourage anyone interested in taking part in the community to read it! 

Xavier's question to you: What is the blog of the community that has taught you or helped you the most (your "MVB - most valuable blog")?

entry.title: George Drakos
entry.content:
More about George Drakos in SAP Community

 

 


Name: George Drakos
Location: Athens, Greece
Occupation: SAP Technical Consultant


George, tell us about yourself.
Who are you, what do you like to do, what is important to you?

I work as an SAP Technical Consultant with over 6.5 years of experience designing and implementing custom SAP solutions, mainly around ABAP, Fiori, ABAP Cloud and clean core principles. My background is in Electrical and Computer Engineering, and during my studies and for a while after I was working on machine learning engineering. I ended up in the SAP world right after finishing my studies. What started as a first step after university turned into something I enjoy doing it every day. 

Outside of work, I spend time at the gym and play electric bass. On the technical side, I really enjoy building things on GitHub and sharing my code, mostly open source tools that are ABAP Cloud compatible. What matters to me the most is helping other developers grow. When I started out I had a lot of help from people around me, and I think SAP is one of those areas where you can't really get far on your own. You don't have easy access to a system to experiment with, the available material is limited, and things move fast with new frameworks like RAP, so it's very easy for someone starting today to feel lost. That's why I try to mentor team members whenever I can and share whatever I've picked up along the way. 


What is your area of expertise?
What does a usual day at work look like for you?

My main area of expertise is ABAP Cloud development, with a strong focus on clean core principles and the RAP framework. I work a lot with object-oriented ABAP and design patterns, which in my experience makes a huge difference when projects start to grow. Lately I've been doing quite a bit of work with RAP, building modern Fiori apps end to end on top of it. I'm industry agnostic, so I've had the chance to work across different domains rather than being tied to one specific sector. 

My day usually starts with going through my emails and prioritizing the tasks I have on my plate, since I'm normally working on several projects in parallel. After that, the biggest chunk of my time goes into coding and debugging, which is honestly the part of the job I enjoy the most. The rest of the day is a mix of things: leading the technical design on the projects I'm involved in, doing peer reviews, and tuning programs when something needs to run faster. What really keeps me engaged is when I get a task that actually delivers real value to the users and at the same time pushes me to dig around and figure out the best way to implement it.Those are the ones that keep the job interesting. 


What inspired you to become an SAP Champion in SAP Community?

For me, being an SAP Champion is essentially a bridge between the technology and the people. You don't have to be the smartest developer in the room who knows every single detail. It's more about being approachable and showing up consistently for others. I wanted to be that person who, when someone asks what they're afraid is a "stupid" question, takes the time to answer properly and makes them feel welcome.Nobody really pushed me towards it. I just wanted to give back to the community the same way people helped me when I was starting out, and the Champion program felt like the right way to do that on a bigger scale. 


How do you learn in SAP Community?
What is your daily/weekly routine in SAP Community?

Apart from the SAP Community itself, I keep a close eye on the latest blog posts and articles being shared on LinkedIn, and I also have 3-4 favourite websites that I check regularly. Putting these sources together givesme a pretty good picture of what's going on in the SAP world at any given moment. My routine starts with checking the latest blog posts in the SAP Community. But for me the process doesn't stop at just reading and understanding the concepts. I always try to implement them hands-on, because I strongly believe that to really get something, you have to apply it yourself. So my daily and weekly rhythm is basically a loop: I find something new in the community, and then I jump straight into the system to try it out. 


What is your suggestion to get the most out of SAP Community?

My biggest suggestion is to not just be a passive reader. Try things out in practice, don't hesitate to ask questions even if you think they might be too simple, and jump into the answers when you can. Engaging in discussions and sharing your own hands-on experiences, both the wins and the roadblocks, is the fastest way to learn and build real connections with other professionals. One more thing, especially for ABAP developers: be open to sharing your code on GitHub. It's something our community has been a bit slow to embrace compared to other tech ecosystems, but it makes a huge difference, both for the people who learn from your repos and for you when you get feedback on your work. 

A question to you, from George:
Is there an SAP feature or framework that you think is underrated, and more developers should be using?

entry.title: Yogananda Muthaiah
entry.content:
More about Yogananda Muthaiah in SAP Community

 

 


Name: Yogananda Muthaiah  
Location: Munich, Germany
Occupation: Senior Technology Consultant at SAP


Yogananda, tell us about yourself.
Who are you, what do you like to do, what is important to you?

I am Yogananda and I live in Munich, Germany. I’ve been working in SAP for more than 8+yrs as a Senior Technology Consultant. I’m more passionate about new technologies, collaboration, and continuous learning. I enjoy sharing knowledge, mentoring, and building strong connections within the SAP ecosystem. What matters most to me is helping others succeed and contributing to a thriving SAP community. 

I am a technology enthusiast who loves exploring new innovations in enterprise software and preparing for success on customer projects to Go-live. Outside of work, I enjoy problem-solving, engaging with community members, and staying curious about emerging trends (currently on AI). Knowledge-sharing and collaboration are important to me because they empower individuals and strengthen the SAP Community. 


What is your area of expertise?
What does a usual day at work look like for you?

My expertise is currently in various product areas (SAP BTP, SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP Integration Suite, CAP, SAP UI5, SAP CPQ, SAP Incentive Management. etc)  and enterprise solutions, with a focus on customer implementation, design & development, and guiding customers through their SAP transformation journeys. I specialize in helping customers leverage SAP tools effectively to drive business outcomes. 

My typical day involves collaborating with internal colleagues, customers, and sometimes with Partners solving technical challenges and working on SAP projects. I spend time researching new features, engaging with community content, and applying innovative solutions to real-world business problems. 


What inspired you to become an SAP Champion in SAP Community?

I was inspired by the spirit of collaboration and knowledge exchange in the SAP Community. Seeing how impactful shared experiences and mentorship can motivate me to contribute actively and help others grow in their SAP careers. 


How do you learn in SAP Community?
What is your daily/weekly routine in SAP Community?

II learn by reading blogs, Q&A discussions, and tutorials, attending webinars, and engaging in conversations with experts. The diversity of perspectives in the community helps me stay updated and continuously improve my skills. 

Daily: Checking questions, answering queries, and reading blogs.
Weekly: Participating in discussions, contributing insights, and sharing my own experiences through blogs or comments.
Regularly: Networking with peers and exploring new SAP topics to stay ahead. 


What is your suggestion to get the most out of SAP Community?

My key advice is simple — engage actively. Ask questions, share your experiences, and contribute meaningfully to discussions. The more consistent you participate, the more you learn, grow, and build valuable relationships. Consistency, curiosity, and a collaborative mindset are the cornerstones of making the most of what the SAP Community has to offer. 

Yogananda's question to you: What is one SAP Community connection or collaboration that has had the biggest impact on your professional journey — and what made it so significant?

entry.title: Re: For restricting edit/create options for Ariba purchas...
entry.content:
Some of the recommended approaches to meet your requirement

1. Restrict App Access
The simplest safeguard is to remove access to these apps for roles that should not touch Ariba PRs/POs. Create separate business roles:

  • One with access to standard PR/PO apps (with M_BEST_BSA restrictions).
  • Another without access to mass change apps.

This way, only a small group of trusted users can run mass changes.

2. Custom Business Catalog / Role Restriction
Build a custom business catalog that excludes these apps for most users. Assign the catalog only to users who need mass change functionality for non‑Ariba document types.

3. Extensibility / Custom Logic
Use the Business Add‑In (BAdI) for Purchase Requisition/Order Validation (released in Public Cloud) to enforce a check if document type = ZAR (or ARB), block the change/save action. This gives you a programmatic safeguard even if the app is accessible.

4. Workflow / Output Control
As a fallback, you can configure workflow or output determination to raise an error or block processing if a mass change is attempted on restricted document types. This does not prevent the initial edit but stops the transaction from completing.

entry.title: Re: BusinessObjects 移行検証用の一時ライセンス取得について
entry.content:
ご参照いただいた KBA 1288121 は、BOBJ 製品の有効な契約が失効した顧客が緊急ライセンスキー（Emergency Key）を申請するための内容となっております。
バックエンドで確認したところ、貴社には BOBJ 製品の有効な契約が存在しております。そのため、BOBJ の永久ライセンスキー（Permanent License Key） を申請可能であり、緊急ライセンスキーは必要ございません。
 
BOBJ の永久ライセンスキーの申請方法につきましては、該当 KBA をご参照ください。
1502717 - How to request a license key for SAP BusinessObjects Solutions - SAP for Me 
また、参考用に操作動画も作成いたしました。
 
ライセンスマテリアル の選択に関してご不明な点があれば、以下のKBA をご確認ください。

1794184 - What are the different license types (NUL, CAL, CS, CSBL, CPU) for SAP BusinessObjects products

それ以上の質問があれば、貴社の営業担当者にお気軽に問い合わせください。

entry.title: SAP CPI WorkMate — Chrome Extension to View Data Store and JMS Payloads Directly
entry.content:
You are debugging an integration flow and want to view a message from a Data Store or a JMS Queue to see what's inside the payload? Here's what that currently looks like:

  1. Open Monitor → Data Stores (or Message Queues)
  2. Find the entry
  3. Downalod it as ZIP file
  4. Extract the Zip
  5. Open the files in a text editor of your choice
  6. Manually format the JSON or XML to make it readable

That's six steps — just to read a message payload. Imagine doing this multipe times a Day!!

Introducing SAP CPI WorkMate

SAP CPI WorkMate is a free Chrome extension that cuts all of that down to a fewer click.

It injects a View Data button directly into the SAP Integration Suite monitoring pages — both Data Stores and JMS Message Queues. You select a row, click the button, and the payload opens instantly in a built-in viewer right in your browser.

   The extension uses your existing browser session, so there's no separate login or token to manage.

What You Get

Instant payload viewing
Click any entry row in Data Stores or any message in JMS Queues, hit View Data, and the payload is fetched, unzipped, and displayed — all in the browser.

Syntax highlighting
JSON and XML payloads are colour-coded out of the box, making it easy to scan large messages at a glance.

Auto Format
Turn on Auto Format in settings and payloads are automatically pretty-printed the moment they load. Or keep it off and format manually when you need it.

Full-text search
Use the search bar inside the viewer to find a specific field or value across the payload. Navigate between matches with Prev / Next.

Line numbers
Every file shows line numbers alongside the content — handy when you're referencing a specific line in a large XML document.

Multiple files in one view
Data Store or JMS payloads often contain more than one file inside the zip (body, headers, properties). The viewer shows all of them in a left sidebar — click any file to switch.

Copy and Download
Copy the current file to clipboard with one click, or download it directly to your machine.

Configurable appearance
Choose between Dark and Light themes, three overlay sizes, and three font sizes — all saved across sessions via the extension settings popup.

How to Install

Find SAP CPI Workmate in Chrome Web Store and click Add to Chrome.

How to Use

For Data Stores:
1. Navigate to Monitor → Data Stores
2. Click a data store in the left panel
3. Click any entry row to select it
4. Click the View Data button that appears next to the Filter by ID field
5. Browse the payload files in the left sidebar of the viewer

For JMS Message Queues:
1. Navigate to Monitor → Message Queues
2. Click a queue in the left panel
3. Click any message row to select it
4. Click View Data

The View Data button is automatically greyed out when no row is selected, and resets when you switch to a different data store or queue — so you always know you're viewing the right payload.

A Note on Security

The extension does not collect, store, or transmit any data outside your browser. All API calls go directly to your *.hana.ondemand.com tenant using your existing browser session. Nothing is sent to any third-party server.

Links

Chrome Web Store: SAP CPI Workmate
GitHub: SAP-CPI-Workmate
License: MIT — free to use, modify, and distribute

If you run into any issues or have suggestions for features, feel free to leave a comment below or open an issue on GitHub.
