entry.title: LDAP Authentication Configuration in SAP HANA 2.0.
entry.content:
Purpose.


This blog describes the steps to configure LDAP‑based authentication for SAP HANA 2.0.  LDAP‑based authentication is helpful to users for the reasons listed below.


1. When there are many HANA databases in your landscape remembering separate passwords for each databases is becomes difficult.
2. Users can log in to all HANA databases using one set of password.

 

Overview.

LDAP user authentication was first introduced in HANA 2.0 SPS 00.
if your database is on HANA 2.0. You can configure LDAP as per the step listed in this blog. If LDAP is available in your company network. You can take advantage of this feature.

Procedure

To enable LDAP user authentication, a connection to an LDAP server must be established by creating an LDAP provider in the SAP HANA database.

 

Prerequisites.

Team Requirement for this activity.

1. AD Team---> Will Create Service User in AD. We have SAPD1FUME.

This ID 10319259 in Active Directory and with this ID we will login in HANA DB from Studio level after LDAP Configuration.

2. Azure AD Team---> Will Set the SPN.

3. Security Team---> Will create Role and Authorization.

4. Basis Team----> will do the LDAP Configuration.

5. We need to this Setup in System DB and Tenant DB.

6. You have the system privilege LDAP ADMIN.

 

Step1.

Now login in AD and check Service Account Created or not.

 

 

Step2.

Now login in SYSTEM DB in Studio level and run below query.

 

We are creating LDAP Provider from below query in SYSTEM DB .

 

SAPD1FUME is Service Account created by AD Team.

CREATE LDAP PROVIDER DD_LDAP CREDENTIAL TYPE 'PASSWORD' USING 'user=CN=SAPD1FUME,OU=Service Accts,OU=Global,OU=DDUsers,DC=DDX,DC=com;password=9]@85046[@LC666' USER LOOKUP URL 'ldaps://is0gbdf6dc03.ddx.com.ddx.com:636/OU=DDUsers,DC=DDX,DC=com??sub?(&(objectClass=user)(sAMAccountName=*))' ATTRIBUTE DN 'distinguishedName' ATTRIBUTE MEMBER_OF 'memberOf' SSL OFF DEFAULT ON ENABLE PROVIDER;

 

Step3.

 

Now creating in Tenant DB.

CREATE LDAP PROVIDER DD_LDAP CREDENTIAL TYPE 'PASSWORD' USING 'user=CN=SAPD1FUME,OU=Service Accts,OU=Global,OU=DDUsers,DC=DDX,DC=com;password=9]@85046[@LC666' USER LOOKUP URL 'ldaps://pw01bdh6dc01.ddx.com:636/OU=BDUsers,DC=DDX,DC=com??sub?(&(objectClass=user)(sAMAccountName=*))' ATTRIBUTE DN 'distinguishedName' ATTRIBUTE MEMBER_OF 'memberOf' SSL OFF DEFAULT ON ENABLE PROVIDER;

 

Step4.

 

Now you should have root CA Certificate. If you don’t have Download from below.

Go go Lock symbol and click on Certificate (Vaild)

 

 

Step5.

Secure Communication between SAP HANA and an LDAP Directory Server

Now Login in HANA Cockpit and Add your SYSTEM and TENANT DB. Go to Security Related Links on both SYSTEM DB and Tenant DB.  Login to SYSTEM Database in HANA Cockpit > “Security Related Links” >Click on Certificate Store

   Import root certificate here which we exported earlier step.

 

 

Now go to Certificate Collections

Click on Add Collection.

 

Give here any name 

 

Now Add Certificate.

 

Click ok.

Now Click on Edit Purpose.

Save.

 

 

 

Step6.

Now do same steps in Tenant DB which you done in  (Step5) SYSTEM DB.

Login to TENANT Database in HANA Cockpit > “Security Related Links” >Click on Certificate Store.

 

Step7.

Now Validate the LDAP on Both SYSTEM and TENANT DB.

VALIDATE LDAP PROVIDER DD_LDAP;

    SYSTEM DB.

 

Step8.

Tenant DB.

 

We just VALIDATED LDAP PROVIDER in SYSTEM and Tenant DB.

 

Step9.

Now LDAP Group Authorization.

Here we are Assigning MONITORING Role to LDAP Group CN=SG-SAPHANAadmin&dev,

ALTER ROLE "MONITORING" ADD LDAP GROUP 'CN=SG-SAPHANAadmin&dev,OU=Global,OU=DDGroups,DC=DDX,DC=com';

 

Step10.

Same Query you need to run in Tenant DB.

 

 

Step11.

Now run this query in SYSTEM DB and then Tenant DB.

ALTER LDAP PROVIDER DD_LDAP ENABLE USER CREATION FOR LDAP;

 

 

Step12.

Now Add your ID in SYSTEM and Tenant DB.

Step13.

This 10319259 ID is created in AD LADAP and i am able to login in HANA DB from HANA Studio.

 

 

Conclusion.
LDAP authentication in SAP HANA allows you to manage users centrally by connecting HANA.

We should leverage Active Directory to configure LDAP-based authentication in SAP HANA.

entry.title: UDF Field Tab Order Navigation Issue in SAP Business One
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

We are facing an issue with the tab navigation order of User Defined Fields (UDFs) in SAP Business One.

Initially, during development, the UDFs were created in a different order without considering the final tab sequence requirement. Later, additional UDFs were added and some field arrangements were changed based on business requirements.

Now, when users enter data and press the Tab key, the cursor does not move to the next logical field. Instead, it jumps between UDF fields in an unexpected sequence.

For example:

Expected Order:
UDF1 to UDF2 to UDF3 to UDF4

Actual Order:
UDF1 to UDF4 to DF2 to UDF3

Since there are already existing records and the UDFs are actively being used in production, recreating the UDFs is not a preferred option.

Could you please advise:

  1. How SAP Business One determines the tab order for UDF fields?
  2. Can the tab sequence of existing UDFs be modified?
  3. Is there any supported method, SAP Note, or workaround to rearrange the tab order without recreating the UDFs?
  4. Has anyone faced a similar issue and found a solution?

Environment:

  • SAP Business One Version: 10
  • Database: SQL Server

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Create a TCode for a Table in SAP ABAP
entry.content:
In SAP ABAP, creating a custom transaction code (TCode) for a table allows users to directly maintain or display table entries without remembering the standard transaction names.

Prerequisites :

Before creating a TCode:

  • A custom table should already exist in the Data Dictionary.
  • Example table: ZVA_EMP_20
  • Proper authorization to create transactions.

Create a Table Maintenance Generator( TMG ) :

  • Go to se11 Transaction Code.
  • Enter your custom Table name (  Ex : ZVA_EMP_20 ).
  • Click on Display.
  • From the top menu  ( Utilities → Table Maintenance Generator ).

  • Click on Table Maintenance Generator .

  •  Click on find screen no.

  • Select propose screen number and click on ok.
  • Now Click on create button then  TMG Generated.

  • SAP generates:

    • Function group
    • Maintenance screens
    • Standard maintenance program

Test Table Maintenance : 

  • Go to SM30 Tcode .
  • Enter a Table Name and Click on Maintain.

 

Create a Custom Transaction Code :

Now create a direct transaction for the table.

  • Open Transaction SE93.
  • Enter custom transaction code ( ex : ZEMP_MAINT ).
  • Click on a Create.
  • Choose Transaction Type is Transaction with parameters (Parameter Transaction).

Now Click on save T-code for Table created .

Providing the Default values also.

 

Finally open table t-code ( ZEMP_MAINT ).

 

Click on Enter.

Now open .

Flow :

SE11
↓
Generate Table Maintenance
↓
SM30 Test
↓
SE93
↓
Create Parameter Transaction
↓
Assign SM30 Parameters
↓
Save & Test

Conclusion :

Creating a T-Code for a custom table in SAP ABAP involves:

  1. Creating a table maintenance generator
  2. Testing using SM30
  3. Creating a parameter transaction in SE93
  4. Passing table parameters to SM30

This approach helps users directly maintain table entries through a simple custom transaction code.

entry.title: Explore SAP Adapters using Discovery Missions
entry.content:
Ever feel like there are plenty of posts about your SAP Adapter yet nothing like a step by step, guided tutorial that allows you to explore the adapter in action. While SAP Blogs and Technology posts generally provide enough to address questions regarding  basic configurations and troubleshooting, if you feel these posts are inadequate, you might be looking in the wrong place.

At the heart of SAP Discovery Center are Missions which are a curated, hands-on journey experience that allow you to build and explore your integration scenarios block by block.

What is SAP Discovery Center?

The SAP Discovery Center is SAP's official learning and enablement hub for SAP Business Technology Platform. To browse missions hosted on SAP you only need a free SAP Universal ID. 

Using Discovery Missions 

Unlike generic tutorials, every mission is scoped to a specific outcome. I was able to find specific missions for SAP S/4HANA to a third-party system using SAP Integration Suite.

  • Each mission has a graphical board  which has dedicated sections for pre-requisites, authentication setups, and detailed configurations that allow you to get a modular understanding of the entire mission.
  • You can mark a section as complete and it will allow you to view your progress on the dashboard. Really handy for someone like me who gets overwhelmed if an integration use case takes too long !

Why Missions work : User Oriented Tutorials

Every Discovery Mission is user oriented. This means you can pick up any mission and start exploring it at random and you will be guided through every step of the mission setup. What you can expect in a mission:

  • A single, measurable outcome defined before step one. No ambiguity about when you are done.
  • Each tile in your project board contains no more than a few steps related to the tile header.
  • There are screenshots which help you follow along, giving you the confidence to progress.

Need Further Support?

  • Discovery Missions allow you to connect with others, who have tried similar missions.
  • Many Missions are fully guided, meaning you can drop a comment to the Product Expert who will help you in case you get stuck.

Best Practices

  • Open BAH alongside the Mission: The SAP Business Accelerator Hub  is a great aid while navigating through adapter missions. When a step says "Configure the Jira adapter," having the adapter's official API spec open in a second tab makes parameter choices instantly clear.
  • Take notes on credentials and IDs: Missions often ask you to copy a Client ID or Tenant URL that you created in Step 2. Keep a scratch doc open. Nothing derails a mission faster than hunting for an OAuth secret you generated 45 minutes ago.
  • Join the SAP Community thread for the Mission: Every mission links to a Community discussion thread. If you get stuck, someone else almost certainly hit the same wall and the answer is already there, often from the mission author themselves.

entry.title: Enhancing SAP APIM Monitoring with Logging and Automated Email Alerts Using SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Introduction  

In SAP API Management (APIM), monitoring and troubleshooting policy-level failures can be challenging. At API Monitoring Dashboard, we can check analytics data and check the response code for each API call. However, when a call fails at policy level, there is no visibility of request payload. Also, the support team must check manually on the APIM dashboard for policy failure.  

To address this limitation, a custom logging and alerting framework has been implemented by leveraging SAP Cloud Integration. This approach enables logging of policy-level failures at API level, improved payload visibility, and real-time alert generation for better operational monitoring and faster issue resolution.  

In this blog post, I will walk through a practical approach to building a custom logging and alerting solution for APIM using SAP Cloud Integration.  

Step-by-Step Guide  

In this blog, I assume that the API proxy is already created in SAP API Management (APIM), and you are now applying policies to the API proxy flow. For example, you may already have policies such as VerifyAPIKey, Quota, other Security Policies, Mediation, or Traffic Management policies configured in your API flow.  

The objective is to capture failures occurring at the policy level, log the required details, and trigger alerts using SAP Cloud Integration.  

Suppose you have applied policies mentioned below to your API and API fails at VerifyAPIKey policy.  

When API is triggered and it failed at VerifyAPIKey policy :  Mail alert and Logging of payload will be triggered from APIM.  

Policies required to achieve this functionality:  

Key Value Maps: To fetch credentials for Cloud Integration.  

Basic Auth: To authenticate Cloud Integration flow endpoint. 

Service Callout: To call CIoud Integration flow and pass headers like resource path and payload to Cloud Integration. Also, Service Callout policy will be triggered based on the condition at Policy and does not interfere with the normal API execution path.  

 Service Callout Policy Content  

<ServiceCallout xmlns="http://www.sap.com/apimgmt" async="true" continueOnError="true" enabled="true">  
<Request variable="myRequest">  
<Set>  
<Headers>  
<Header name="Authorization">{Auth}</Header>  
<Header name="Resource">{proxy.pathsuffix}</Header>  
</Headers>  
<Payload contentType="text/plain" variablePrefix="@"  
   variableSuffix="#">@request.content#  
</Payload>  
</Set>  
</Request>  
<Response>GeocodingResponse</Response>  
<Timeout>300000</Timeout>  
<HTTPTargetConnection>  
<!--  The URL to the service being called  -->  
<URL>https://{CIUrl}</URL>  
<SSLInfo>  
<Enabled>true</Enabled>  
<ClientAuthEnabled>false</ClientAuthEnabled>  
<KeyStore/>  
<KeyAlias/>  
<TrustStore/>  
<Ciphers/>  
<Protocols/>  
</SSLInfo>  
</HTTPTargetConnection>  
</ServiceCallout>

 Mail alert and logging flow will be triggered in Cloud Integration. The request payload is sent as a mail attachment for a failed message.  

Logs the payload and is visible in Monitor Message Processing as an attachment.

  

Conclusion  

Logging and mail alerting for SAP API Management using SAP Cloud Integration helps improve monitoring, issue resolution, and overall operational efficiency. Following the step-by-step approach outlined in this blog, organizations can proactively track API failures, capture request payload, and receive timely email notifications for critical events.  

This solution not only enhances visibility into API executions but also reduces manual effort in troubleshooting and monitoring production issues. With proper logging and automated alerts in place, support teams can respond faster, minimize downtime, and ensure a more reliable integration landscape.  

Please Note:  This solution is applicable to any SAP APIM deployment where multiple policies are applied to API proxy flows, visibility into policy-level failures is required and proactive alerting to support teams along with Request Payload attachment is a business requirement.

entry.content:
Hi. Thank you for the response. We are using 2023 Private Cloud version. The unit of measure “H” is also not visible in the T006 table. I had already discussed this issue with the MM consultants but their response was that “H” is not a format we use. Instead of “H”, we use “SA” or “ST”. When I checked Go To → Translations → Unit of Measurements, nothing was selected for EN or TR. I will ask the MM consultant to change it.

If the system had not allowed postings with “SA”, then the real issue could indeed have been the missing “H” unit of measure. Of course, this is just my opinion. Maybe the system allows the record to be created even if the “H” unit is not maintained. Also, I would like to mention that with F2173(Find Maintenance Order and Operation app) and IW41, the system allows posting without any errors.

entry.title: Develop RAP-Based SAP Fiori Applications in Minutes with SAP Fiori Tools AI Project Accelerator
entry.content:
Last year, as described in the blog From idea to app: using AI-powered Project Accelerator to develop SAP Fiori apps, the Project Accelerator focused on generating CAP-based projects,

Developing enterprise SAP Fiori applications using the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) provides a standardized way to build modern cloud-ready applications. These applications are typically developed in environments such as SAP BTP ABAP Environment and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (version 2602 or higher).

In a significant step forward, the Project Accelerator now supports RAP-based projects In this post, I will explain why the addition of RAP is important and then describe the specific steps  to build an app using this new capability.

The addition of RAP support makes the AI Project Accelerator more relevant for enterprise SAP development by aligning it with SAP’s strategic ABAP model. It enables end-to-end generation of backend artifacts and frontend fully functional SAP Fiori elements application including manifest and annotation configurations. Furthermore, it supports the Clean Core 4-Level Extension Model, as the generated backend artifacts fall under Level A—the cleanest form of extensions.

The AI-powered Project Accelerator in SAP Fiori tools is available in SAP Business Application Studio (SBAS) under a SAP Build Code subscription ( is available).

Application ideas or business requirements can be provided by developers, designers, and business experts in various forms, such as:

  1. Text
  2. Image or UI mock-up (.jpg, .png)
  3. Combination of text and image as a business requirement document (.docx, .md)

The project accelerator enables rapid prototyping of a fully functional application that can be showcased and then extended using SAP Fiori tools or any coding assistants (such as Cline, Claude, and Copilot) integrated with MCP servers (for example, MCP server for SAP Fiori or CAP) for further development.

It generates the complete application stack from scratch, including the CDS data model, behavior definitions, service definitions, OData V4 service, and an SAP Fiori elements application. This follows a greenfield approach, with no reuse of existing business objects.

Getting Started

Now that we understand the AI Project Accelerator, let’s proceed with the step-by-step walkthrough to generate the RAP project using an SAP BTP trial account.

Before we begin, complete the following prerequisites:

  1. Get an Account on SAP BTP Trial (follow step 1 to step 5 and in step 6, choose region US East- AWS) . Note: The trial version of SAP Build Code is currently only available in the US East - AWS region.
  2. Start Booster- Prepare an Account for ABAP Trial (only step 1)
  3. Start Booster – Get started with SAP Build Code

Navigate to subaccount(trial) and verify Instances and subscriptions.

Create a Destination in the SAP BTP Cockpit.

Manually add below additional properties to destination and continue to review step.

  • WebIDEEnabled: true
  • abap_enabled: true
  • WebIDEUsage: odata_abap,dev_abap,abap_cloud
  • HTML5.DynamicDestination: true
  • HTML5.SetXForwardedHeaders: false
  • HTML5.Timeout: 360000
Verify all the details of destination and click on create.    
 

Configure ABAP Development Tools (ADT) and create new ABAP package.

  1. You have installed ABAP Development Tools, latest version
  2. Create new ABAP Cloud Project.
  3. Copy URL from destination properties.
  4. Create new ABAP package Z_<Unique Name> under super package ZLOCAL

SAP Build Code - Create new dev space

  1. Open the SAP Build Code lobby.
 
 
 
Create new Dev space that contains SAP Fiori tools extensions in SAP Business Application Studio.
 
 
Provide a Dev space name (for example: FioriAppDevelopment).
 

Once the Dev space is created and starts running, click on FioriAppDevelopment to open SAP Business Application Studio

Launching the SAP Fiori Tools AI: Project Accelerator

 Go to Project Explorer view and Click on Open Folder, to open your workspace folder (/home/user/projects).

 
Open the Command Palette

Search for “Fiori application” and select “Fiori: Open Application Generator”.

 
For AI-assisted SAP Fiori app creation click on "Project Accelerator"
 
Select RAP Accelerator.
  • Select the destination.
  • Enter the ABAP repository name
  • Choose the package for back-end artifacts generation
  • Provide a transport request (if required)
  • Not needed for local packages
  • Click Connect to proceed.
 

Generating Your First SAP Fiori Elements Application

 The AI Project Accelerator supports multiple input formats. One powerful approach is generating SAP Fiori application from images.

I have a hand-drawn sketch representing a Manage Purchase Orders application. the expectation from the Project Accelerator is to interpret the visual design and underlying business intent, identify floorplan and generate a RAP based SAP Fiori elements application. The generated application should closely reflect the structure, fields, and layout depicted in the image, converting the sketch into a fully functional List Report and Object Page.

Download manage_purchase_orders.png to your local disk and upload it to the Projects Folder. Drag and drop the image to the projects pane on the left.

Click on Choose File and select the manage_purchase_orders.png

 

You can view the generation logs to understand how SAP Fiori tools AI generates your application and if needed, to gather additional details for troubleshooting. // Add as side-info

Click on Generate. The project accelerator will analyze the image and generate a, including back-end OData V4 service artifacts and front-end artifacts. 

For supported features and known restrictions, refer to the documentation

In ABAP Development Tools (ADT), check the destination system and ensure that all backend OData V4 service artifacts are generated and available in the selected package.

 

. 

 

After the generation process completes, the new project (e.g., ai-created-rap) is added to your workspace, and the Application Information page opens automatically.

On the Application Information page, under Common Tasks, click Preview Application.

  • Select start-mock to preview the application with AI-generated mock data.
  • Alternatively, use the start script to preview the application with the backend; however, the backend will not have any data as it is newly generated.

You will then see the SAP Fiori elements application with a List Report (including a filter bar and a table for purchase orders. Compare the generated SAP Fiori application with the uploaded image to verify how closely the project accelerator has interpreted the floorplan, fields, and structure.

.       

Overall, the AI Project Accelerator for RAP can significantly speed up the development of RAP-based SAP Fiori applications, enabling developers, designers, and business users to transform ideas, requirements, or even sketches into functional enterprise apps within minutes.

References:

Generating an Application with the Project Accelerator Using SAP Fiori Tools AI

Why use the AI-powered Project Accelerator from SAP Fiori tools?

SAP Fiori Tools Update: Elevating Development with AI, Usability, and Flexibility

entry.title: Business Builders at UFRGS: Where Technology Meets Learning
entry.content:
[This text is a copy of the translation of the original Linkedin post in Portuguese which you can find here: Post | LinkedIn ]


Last week I had the opportunity to participate in the organization and execution of Business Builders, a hands-on experience developed in partnership with SAP and the ERPsim Lab - HEC Montréal.
It was a very different and interesting experience because it went beyond theory. During the activity, we truly had to analyze information, interpret data, discuss decisions, and understand how tools used in the market can support strategies within companies.
It was great to see how technology can transform learning into something more practical, interactive, and even fun. This type of activity brings students much closer to market reality and allows us to see, in practice, how data analysis and decision-making show up in the day-to-day of organizations.
I was also very happy to see the level of engagement from everyone throughout the activity. There was great participation, questions, discussion, and constant exchange, which made everything even richer — especially because the entire experience was conducted in English.
I would like to extend my special thanks to Isadora Royer and Eduardo Baptista de Oliveira from SAP, for their outreach, support, and trust throughout the entire organization of the event. And also to Artur Borowski and Cecília Sanches, who worked alongside me on the more administrative and operational side of organizing an event of this scale within the university through the Student Council.
I am very grateful to have been part of all of this and I hope this was just the beginning of more initiatives like this within the Production Engineering community.
#UFRGS #ProductionEngineering #SAP #HECMontreal #BusinessBuilders #DataAnalysis #Engineering

entry.title: Post/Reverse authorization in Supplier Invoice.
entry.content:
First of all, it needs to be made clear that as long as the users can see the Post button in create supplier invoices App, then users can see the Reverse button. By the same logic, if the reverse button is not visible to the user, then this user will also not be able to see the Post button. Because both Post and reverse are theoretically operations of posting invoice, the authorization involved in them cannot be split.

  • If users business requirement is to hide the Post button for a user and user can accept this user without seeing the reverse button, then please refer to the content in this community link. After using the best solution in the community to solve it, the effect of hiding the Post/Reverse button can be achieved.

Community link：https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/how-to-restrict-the-authorization-of-users-to-post-supplier-invoices/qaq-p/14147810

 

  • If users business requirement is to restrict post supplier invoice but can reverse supplier invoice, please refer to KBA 0003719298 "Cannot split the authorization to post and reverse supplier invoices in SAP S/4HANA Cloud", which contains workaround for user reference.

KBA 0003719298 Cannot split the authorization to post and reverse supplier invoices in SAP S/4HANA Cloud

 

Best Regards,

Daisy

entry.title: Re: Picking List
entry.content:
Good day @XavierJ 

Thank you for your question.

Yes, there are limitations as you have notice. The requested storage bin wise sorting cannot be achieved through the current released framework for the Manufacturing Order Pick List output. The form template can be copied and adjusted, and additional supported fields can be exposed through data source extensions or custom fields, but the actual component line sequence is controlled by the standard backend form data provider. See KBA 3550037 and 3485035 for more insights.

I suggest you consider the following steps:

  1. Confirm the Pick List form uses FDP_PP_MOR_PICK_LIST_SRV.
  2. Create a custom Pick List from template only if you require layout or field display changes.
  3. Check whether the storage bin field can be displayed through a supported data source extension.
  4. Test the output from a production order and document that the line order is not controlled by the form.
  5. If storage bin sorting is mandatory in the official Pick List output, then consider raising an incident or an influence request, because your required sorting extension point is currently not available.

 

See the following references for more insights.

3483228 - How to add a standard field on the manufacturing order output - SAP for Me

3484577 - How to add a custom field on the manufacturing order output - SAP for Me

3319747 - [CE2302]Extended data source and custom fields don't show up in the PP output forms in Adobe LiveCycle Designer - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Process Integration Pipeline Extension - Restart via Data Store
entry.content:
@mate_moricz I believe there's a small issue in how the extension handles cases where the receiver determination finds no receiver. It retries the message despite the setting "Error if no receiver is found" that is set in the XSLT. 

You could possibly check header "exceptionCustomStatus" for value "ReceiverNotFoundError" in Iflow "Pipeline DS Retry - Custom Error Handling with Data Stores". Also it should be made sure that the custom status is not lost and overwritten with "AutomaticRetry" as this makes it quite hard for operations to see that the root cause is a failed receiver determination. 

Philippe

entry.title: Re: SAP Signavio announces a new partnership with Tangible Growth
entry.content:
Hi Aaron,

thanks for the question.

Tangible Growth is not intended to replace SAP Signavio Transformation Manager. The two solutions play complementary roles.

Tangible Growth focuses on the strategic planning layer, for example defining and managing strategic priorities, objectives, and key results. SAP Signavio Transformation Manager focuses on connecting those strategic priorities to transformation execution, including initiatives, insights, tasks, value tracking, and progress across the business transformation lifecycle.

The connection between the two is meant to help close the gap between strategy definition and operational execution. In practice, this can mean synchronizing strategic objectives from Tangible Growth into SAP Signavio Transformation Manager and then linking them to transformation initiatives, process insights, and measurable outcomes.

So SAP Signavio Transformation Manager remains the system used to steer and monitor transformation execution. Tangible Growth can provide the upstream strategic context, while SAP Signavio Transformation Manager helps translate that context into concrete initiatives and track whether execution is progressing toward the intended business outcomes.

In short: Tangible Growth helps define and manage strategic direction; SAP Signavio Transformation Manager helps operationalize, connect, and monitor that direction through transformation initiatives.

If you would like to discuss this further or explore potential follow-up topics, feel free to reach out to our shared inbox: signavio_ecosystem@sap.com.

Best,
Alex

entry.title: Re: Conversational Analytics on SAP S/4HANA CDS View Data with AI Claude Code and SAP BusinessObject
entry.content:
Hi Shrenath,
The BOBJ logonToken serves as the authentication credential. It is issued by the BOBJ server after the user’s credentials are successfully verified. Any API request made without this token will return a 401 Unauthorized response. This ensures that only authenticated users can access the data they are authorized to view.

entry.title: Dmitry Kuznetsov
entry.content:
More about Dmitry Kuznetsov in SAP Community

 

 

Name: Dmitry Kuznetsov
Location: Netherlands 
Occupation: SAP S/4HANA Embedded Analytics consultant


Dmitry, tell us about yourself.
Who are you, what do you like to do, what is important to you?

I am an SAP S/4HANA Embedded Analytics consultant, trainer, and certified Databricks data engineer. With 20 years in SAP, I worked across every major data shift—from BW to HANA to Datasphere. I am a lifelong learner, fortunate to have grown alongside SAP’s many technology shifts. I enjoy sharpening my instincts and diving into new challenges—figuratively in tech, and through sailing and kitesurfing.


What is your area of expertise?
What does a usual day at work look like for you?

I specialize in Data & Analytics within the SAP ecosystem. My core expertise includes BW and HANA modeling, along with hands-on work in ABAP CDS and Fiori-based reporting. Recently, I have expanded into Databricks, helping customers migrate and modernize their data platforms.  

My day-to-day work spans consulting and architecture: designing solutions, solving data challenges, and guiding implementations. Alongside this, I teach for SAP Education and regularly speak at conferences of all sizes.


What inspired you to become an SAP Champion in SAP Community?

I have been an active member of the SAP Community for some time and have connected with many SAP Champions along the way. The turning point came at in-person TechEd Berlin 2025, where meeting face-to-face made me realize it was time to step forward and be nominated. I am grateful for the support I received and proud of this recognition. 


How do you learn in SAP Community?
What is your daily/weekly routine in SAP Community?

I learn in the SAP Community by actively engaging: reading blogs, following discussions, and, importantly, answering questions. For me, the community goes beyond SAP-managed platforms. It extends to SIT events and partner-organized SAP insider conferences, where I have been connecting with peers and exchanging ideas for over a decade.


What is your suggestion to get the most out of SAP Community?

Do not limit yourself to online interaction. Seek out local grassroots events—they offer a completely different level of engagement and connection.

Dmitry's question to you:
Why do you need all this data?

entry.title: Re: Enhancement Option for Subcontracting Cockpit in S/4H...
entry.content:
Dear @akash_dutta,

In Public Cloud the Subcontracting Cockpit allows the user at purchasing document item level to either create a delivery of components or directly post goods issues. Therefore for the direct Post Goods Issue scenario the right extension point is the material document item validation BAdI: Check Item Data in Material Document. In SAP documentation this is shown as MMIM_ITEM_CHECK_DATA in the Post Goods Movement extensibility documentation and as BADI_MMIM_CHECK_MATDOC_ITEM in the Material Document extensibility documentation, with business context Material Document Item / MATERIALDOCUMENTITEM

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-sourcing-and-procurement/carrying-out-subcontracting-solution-process

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/85a220ae29a745aab169b630791d0487.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3f57e7df4a114edabffe8b2d581a59ed/12ac3932e65844de911df74ccaf514d5.html

Recommended implementation approach:

1. Create a custom logic implementation for Check Item Data in Material Document / BADI_MMIM_CHECK_MATDOC_ITEM

2. Restrict the logic to the subcontracting PGI scenario based on the fields available in the BAdI interface in the tenant, for example movement type, plant, storage location, batch or special stock indicator

3. Raise an error message when the validation fails so the subcontracting goods issue cannot be posted. For example I’ve prepared Error like below:

IF item_matdoc-GOODSMOVEMENTTYPE = '541'.

  APPEND VALUE #(

    messagetype = 'E'

    messagetext = 'BAdI working - subcontracting PGI validation triggered'

  ) TO messages.

ENDIF.

For delivery-based subcontracting, where the process first creates an outbound delivery and PGI is posted later from delivery processing the delivery BAdI LE_SHP_MODIFY_POST_GM can also be evaluated. SAP positions this BAdI for delivery processing at the interface to goods movement posting

Summary:

For direct PGI from Subcontracting Cockpit use Check Item Data in Material Document / BADI_MMIM_CHECK_MATDOC_ITEM as the central validation point. For delivery-based PGI, additionally evaluate LE_SHP_MODIFY_POST_GM. This keeps the solution aligned with Public Cloud extensibility and avoids modifying the Subcontracting Cockpit UI itself

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Adarsh Rao
entry.content:
More about Adarsh Rao in SAP Community

 


Name: Adarsh Rao
Location: Düsseldorf, Germany
Occupation: Enterprise Integrations and Architecture at SAP


Adarsh, tell us about yourself.
Who are you, what do you like to do, what is important to you?

My name is Adarsh Rao, and I work as a SAP Integration Consultant. My role mainly involves building/ advising customers on connecting different systems and ensuring data flows smoothly across them. I especially enjoy working on complex integration scenarios where there is no straightforward solution. I like learning new things, experimenting with ideas, and sharing practical solutions based on real project experience. 

I enjoy understanding problems deeply and finding practical solutions. I like working on challenges that require a bit of thinking beyond standard approaches. What is important to me is continuous learning, doing quality work, and sharing knowledge so that others can benefit from it as well. 


What is your area of expertise?
What does a usual day at work look like for you?

My main area of expertise is SAP Integration (SAP Integration Suite, SAP PI/PO, SAP XI). I focus on designing, developing, and supporting complex integration scenarios between SAP and non-SAP systems. 
A typical day includes analyzing integration requirements, designing solutions, resolving issues that are not straightforward, and supporting ongoing integrations. I also spend time collaborating with my project team members, documenting solutions, and learning about new SAP features and best practices. 


What inspired you to become an SAP Champion in SAP Community?

While working on complex problems, I often found valuable ideas and solutions shared by others in SAP Community. This inspired me to become an SAP Champion so I could also share my own learnings and help others who face similar non‑standard challenges. 


How do you learn in SAP Community?
What is your daily/weekly routine in SAP Community?

I learn mainly by reading blog posts, exploring different solution approaches, and following experienced community members. I especially learn a lot from posts that explain how real problems were solved in real projects. I regularly follow integration-related content and read new blog posts. Whenever I encounter a problem with no direct solution, I try to share my ideas or approaches based on experience. Weekly, I spend some time actively engaging with content and contributing when I feel my input can help others. 


What is your suggestion to get the most out of SAP Community?

Be curious, be active, and don’t hesitate to share your ideas even if the solution is not perfect. SAP Community becomes most valuable when people share how they solved complex or uncommon problems. 

Adarsh's question to you: What was the most complex problem you solved with the help of SAP Community, and what did you learn from it?
